# Copilot Review Instructions for MetricFlow

This file configures GitHub Copilot's automated review behavior for the `metricflow` repository. All guidance here supplements the human review process described in [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md).

---

## Pre-merge Requirements

The following are non-negotiable gates. Flag violations immediately; a PR that fails any of them should not proceed to human review.

### Issue linkage

PRs that modify an interface must reference an open GitHub issue before implementation. For other substantive changes, such as bug fixes or behavioral changes, issue linkage is encouraged but should not be treated as a hard merge gate based on [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md). If an interface-change PR description contains no issue reference, comment:

> This PR appears to modify an interface but does not reference an open GitHub issue. Per [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md), interface changes should be proposed in an issue and discussed before implementation. Please open or link the relevant issue and allow time for maintainer feedback before requesting review.

For interface changes specifically (see [Interface Changes](#interface-changes) below), the linked issue must also contain evidence that the `@dbt-labs/semantic-layer` codeowner team or an OSI maintainer has acknowledged the proposal before implementation began.

### Contributor License Agreement

If the CLA bot has not verified the contributor, comment:

> The CLA check has not passed. All contributors to MetricFlow must sign the [Contributor License Agreement](https://docs.getdbt.com/community/resources/contributor-license-agreements) before their PR can be merged. If you believe the bot missed your entry, add a comment with `@cla-bot check` to trigger a recheck.

### Changelog entry

Every PR that introduces a user-visible change (new feature, bug fix, behavior change, deprecation) must include a file in `.changes/unreleased/` generated by `changie new`. If this entry is absent and the change is not purely internal (test-only, docs-only, CI-only), comment:

> This PR appears to be missing a `changie` changelog entry. Run `changie new` from the repo root, follow the prompts, and commit the generated file in `.changes/unreleased/`. Do not edit `CHANGELOG.md` directly. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md#adding-or-modifying-a-changelog-entry) for details.

---

## PR Scope and Size

### One PR, one concern

A PR should do one thing. This is the single most important property for receiving a fast, high-quality review. Mixed concerns — a bug fix bundled with a refactor, a new feature alongside unrelated cleanup — make it harder to reason about correctness and harder to revert individual changes if a regression is discovered. When a PR conflates independent concerns, suggest separation:

> This PR appears to combine multiple independent concerns (e.g., _[describe]_). Consider splitting it into focused, independently-mergeable units. Per [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md), a PR that mixes bug fixes, refactors, and new features is significantly harder to review carefully — and the risk of subtle correctness issues slipping through increases.

### Commit structure

Commits should narrate the development arc of the PR. A reviewer should be able to step through the commits in order and follow the reasoning. If a PR involving non-trivial changes is squashed into a single undifferentiated commit, suggest:

> For non-trivial changes, structured commits help reviewers follow your reasoning. Consider organizing your commits so each one represents one logical step — for example, first an internal refactor, then new behavior on top. This is especially important in MetricFlow's compilation and plan-conversion code, where intermediate states are meaningful.

---

## Interface Changes

Flag any PR that modifies the following surfaces as an **interface change** requiring prior discussion and codeowner buy-in:

- **Semantic manifest schema** (`metricflow_semantic_interfaces/`) — this is the OSI standard interface shared across dbt Labs, Snowflake, Tableau, and other partners. Changes here have multi-organization impact and require maintainer approval beyond the standard review process.
- **Query API / public Python interfaces** — any change to public-facing classes, functions, or protocols in `metricflow_semantics/api/` or `metricflow/protocols/`.
- **Metric, dimension, and entity definition semantics** — changes to how semantic manifest objects are interpreted or validated affect every project using MetricFlow.
- **CLI commands and flags** (`dbt-metricflow/`) — flag names, types, and defaults are part of the user-facing contract.
- **Dataflow plan node interfaces** — `metricflow/dataflow/` defines the intermediate representation; changes to node types or their semantics affect all downstream plan-to-SQL conversion.
- **SQL rendering contracts** — changes to how any plan node renders SQL across engines must be verified to preserve correctness on all supported engines.

When an interface change is detected without a linked issue showing prior discussion:

> This PR modifies a MetricFlow interface (specifically: _[identify the area]_). Per [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md#before-you-write-any-code), interface changes require maintainer buy-in before implementation begins — not at review time. If this was discussed in a linked issue, please make that approval visible. If not, please pause this PR, open an issue, and wait for feedback before proceeding.

---

## Semantic Correctness

MetricFlow's core promise is that metrics are computed correctly. Compilation bugs are worse than crashes — they produce silently wrong numbers. Apply heightened scrutiny to any PR that touches:

- Aggregation logic (especially `DISTINCT`, `SUM`, `COUNT`, and measure filtering)
- Join fanout and de-duplication strategies
- Time spine and granularity alignment
- Cumulative metric window frames
- Ratio and expression metric composition
- Conversion metric logic

For changes in these areas, comment if the PR description does not explicitly reason about correctness:

> This PR modifies metric computation logic. Please add an explanation in the PR description of why the new behavior is semantically correct — not just what changed, but why the resulting SQL will produce accurate metric values. A test showing the expected SQL output for at least one affected query shape is required.

---

## Cross-Engine Compatibility

MetricFlow generates SQL for multiple execution engines: DuckDB (the default for local testing), PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks, and Trino. SQL that is valid on one engine is not necessarily valid on all.

When a PR modifies SQL generation, rendering, or plan conversion:

- Verify that any SQL syntax used is engine-agnostic, or is correctly isolated behind an engine-specific rendering path.
- Changes that hard-code dialect-specific syntax (window function syntax, date arithmetic, quoting conventions, `QUALIFY`, `ILIKE`, etc.) outside of engine-specific renderers are a defect.
- If a contributor has only tested against one engine (commonly DuckDB), note which engines require validation before merge.

If engine-specific SQL appears in a shared rendering path:

> SQL in this path is executed across all supported engines. The syntax introduced here (_[describe]_) may not be valid on all targets. Engine-specific behavior must be isolated in the appropriate engine rendering class or behind a dialect check. Please verify compatibility or explain why this syntax is universally supported.

---

## Test Snapshot Management

MetricFlow uses snapshot-based SQL testing extensively. Tests in `tests_metricflow/` compare generated SQL against committed snapshots. If a PR modifies SQL generation — in any path, for any reason — the snapshots must be regenerated and committed.

If a PR modifies query compilation or SQL rendering but does not update snapshot files:

> This PR changes SQL generation logic but does not include updated test snapshots. Run `make regenerate-test-snapshots` to regenerate the snapshots for all affected engines, review the diffs to confirm the changes are intentional and correct, and commit the updated files. Snapshots are the primary record of SQL output correctness.

Conversely, if snapshots change but the PR description does not explain why:

> Test snapshots have changed in this PR. Please describe in the PR description what SQL output changed and why the new output is correct. Unexamined snapshot changes are a common source of correctness regressions.

---

## Code Quality

### Type annotations

All new and modified public functions, methods, and class attributes must have [PEP 484 type annotations](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html). MetricFlow enforces this with `mypy`. If a PR introduces unannotated public-facing code:

> `[function/method name]` is missing type annotations. MetricFlow uses `mypy` for static analysis, and all public interfaces must be annotated. Annotate all parameters and return types. Unannotated code will fail the lint gate.

### Lint compliance

All PRs must pass `make lint` (Black, Ruff, and MyPy) before review. If CI is failing lint or type checks, comment on the specific violations rather than asking the author to "fix CI" generically. Formatting issues caught by `black` do not require a comment — they are mechanical and can be fixed in one commit.

### Test coverage

- Bug fixes must include a regression test that would have failed before the fix.
- New features must include unit tests and, for anything that touches SQL generation, at least one snapshot test demonstrating expected output.
- Tests for pure Python logic belong in `tests_metricflow_semantics/` or `tests_metricflow_semantic_interfaces/`.
- Tests that exercise query compilation or SQL generation belong in `tests_metricflow/`.

If a PR adds behavior or fixes a defect without tests:

> This PR does not appear to include tests. Per [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md), PRs that add new behavior or fix bugs must include tests. Please add the appropriate coverage — unit tests for logic that does not require SQL execution, and snapshot/integration tests for any SQL generation changes.

---

## Package Boundary Respect

The repository is organized into three primary packages with distinct roles:

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `metricflow_semantic_interfaces/` | OSI standard schema — semantic metadata representations shared across organizations |
| `metricflow_semantics/` | Semantic layer reasoning — spec resolution, query building, filtering, naming |
| `metricflow/` | Execution engine — dataflow plan construction, plan-to-SQL conversion, query execution |

Changes should respect these boundaries. If a PR introduces dependencies that flow "upward" (e.g., `metricflow/` importing from `metricflow_semantic_interfaces/` in a way that encodes engine-specific concerns, or `metricflow_semantics/` taking on execution-layer responsibilities), flag it:

> This change appears to introduce a dependency or concern across package boundaries. `metricflow_semantic_interfaces/` should remain engine-agnostic and execution-agnostic. `metricflow_semantics/` should not take on execution concerns. Please verify the change is placed in the appropriate package and does not create circular or inappropriate coupling.

---

## TENETS Alignment

MetricFlow's [design tenets](../TENETS.md) establish an ordered priority for SQL construction:

1. **Correctness** — the metric logic must be right
2. **Performance** — SQL should be efficient on the target compute platform
3. **Legibility** — generated SQL should be readable by an analyst
4. **Ease of manipulation** — the code that generates SQL should be maintainable

When a PR makes tradeoffs between these — for example, optimizing generated SQL at the cost of readability, or simplifying the generation code in a way that degrades output legibility — the PR description should explicitly acknowledge the tradeoff and explain why it is acceptable. If no such justification is present:

> This change appears to involve a tradeoff between MetricFlow's design tenets (correctness > performance > legibility > manipulability). Please describe in the PR description which tenets are in tension here and how you resolved the tradeoff. This is especially important for changes to plan-to-SQL conversion and dataflow node logic.

---

## Performance in Compilation Paths

MetricFlow compiles queries against potentially large semantic graphs. Algorithmic complexity in hot paths — plan construction, join resolution, node traversal — has a real impact on real users with large projects.

When a PR introduces new iteration over graph nodes, plan nodes, or instance sets, check for:

- Nested loops over large collections where a set lookup would suffice
- Repeated traversal of the same subgraph without memoization
- Linear scans over sorted or indexed structures

If an O(n²) or worse pattern appears in a compilation path without justification:

> This introduces a nested iteration pattern in a compilation hot path. For large semantic graphs, this may have meaningful performance impact. Please either justify why this is bounded in practice, add a complexity note to the code, or consider an alternative that avoids the quadratic behavior.

---

## Adapter Scope Boundary

`metricflow/` is a query compiler and SQL renderer. It is not an adapter layer. If a PR introduces warehouse-specific connection management, credential handling, or execution logic that should live in `dbt-adapters` or the dbt Cloud platform, redirect it:

> This change introduces logic that is specific to executing queries against a particular warehouse. MetricFlow's scope is query compilation and SQL rendering, not warehouse connection management. Please evaluate whether this belongs in the appropriate adapter package or execution layer instead.

---

## Review Comment Standards

- Be specific. Cite line numbers, function names, or test names. Vague observations ("this could be improved") are not actionable.
- Distinguish blocking issues from suggestions. Prefix non-blocking comments with `nit:` or `suggestion:`.
- When flagging a problem, propose a concrete resolution — not just a description of the issue.
- Do not re-raise issues that have already been addressed and marked resolved in the thread.

---

## Branch and Target

All PRs from community contributors must target `main`. If a PR targets any other branch directly, flag it:

> Community PRs should target `main`. If this fix is needed for a prior release, a maintainer will handle the backport after merge. Please retarget this PR to `main`.

---

## Out of Scope

Do not review or comment on:

- Code formatting that is already enforced by `black` — these are caught by the lint gate automatically.
- Stylistic preferences not codified in `ruff.toml` or `mypy.ini`.
- Design decisions that have already received maintainer approval in a linked issue and are faithfully reflected in the implementation.
